title: SEGMENT 2 — Fleming's warning: the original Cassandra

lines.text: SEGMENT 2 — Fleming's warning: the original Cassandra
December 11, 1945, Stockholm.
Alexander Fleming has just received the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine, sharing it with Howard Florey and Ernst Chain, for the discovery and development of penicillin.
He gives his Nobel lecture, a fairly standard scientific recitation of how the drug was found and developed, and toward the end of it he delivers a warning that has aged with terrifying accuracy.
Let me paraphrase him, because the actual phrasing is worth holding onto.
He says, in effect, that it is not difficult to make microbes resistant to penicillin in the laboratory.
You simply expose them to concentrations not sufficient to kill them, and you teach them to survive.
And he then warns that the same thing will inevitably happen in the body of the patient, in the home of the buyer of the drug, when penicillin becomes available without prescription.

A man, he says, gives himself an underdose, the more resistant bugs grow up, are passed on to another person, on to another, until they reach someone who develops a real infection from these now-resistant organisms, and that person dies.
The moral, Fleming says, is that you cannot trifle with penicillin.
That is, essentially, 1945.
Penicillin had been mass-produced for two years.
The Nobel speech was given before the drug was even fully rolled out to civilian populations.
And Fleming, the man who discovered it, in the moment of his greatest professional triumph, stood up in Stockholm and told the world precisely what we were about to do to ourselves.
The original Cassandra of antibiotic resistance.
He said it clearly.
We did it anyway.
I want you to sit with the timing of this, because it is the moral spine of the whole lecture.
Fleming did not have to wait fifty years for resistance to be a problem before warning about it.
He saw it in 1945, at the moment of the drug's victory, because he understood evolution by natural selection well enough to predict what selective pressure would do.
Resistance is not an unforeseen complication of antibiotic use.
It is the foreseeable consequence of antibiotic use, predicted at the outset by the discoverer himself.

Every public-health failure that has followed since — over-prescription, over-the-counter sales, agricultural feed additives, hospital under-stewardship — is a failure of will, not a failure of foresight.
We were warned.
We knew.
We did it anyway.
That is a different kind of indictment than "we didn't see this coming.
And the prediction came true on Fleming's exact timeline.
Penicillin's first major clinical use was in 1942 — wounded soldiers, then civilians.
By 1942, the first reports of penicillin-resistant Staph aureus were appearing in the literature.
By 1944, resistance was sufficiently common that physicians were noticing treatment failures.
By the late 1940s and early 1950s, penicillin-resistant Staph — carrying a beta-lactamase enzyme we'll meet in detail later — was the dominant clinical phenotype in hospital strains.
The drug that had been miraculous in 1942 was failing for a substantial fraction of staphylococcal infections by 1952.
Ten years from miracle to crisis, for a drug we thought would solve infection forever.
That is the speed of evolution under selection.
Now, here's the thing I want you to understand about why the resistance was so fast.
It was not because penicillin somehow induced mutations in Staph aureus — drugs don't, generally, work that way.
The resistance was already there, at low frequency, in the bacterial population.

A small fraction of staphylococci carried a beta-lactamase enzyme, originally evolved for who-knows-what purpose — perhaps to detoxify naturally occurring beta-lactams in the soil environment from which these organisms had originally come.
In the absence of penicillin selection, the beta-lactamase gene cost a little to carry and was kept rare.
Add penicillin to the environment — add it everywhere, every hospital, every home — and now that gene confers an enormous fitness advantage.
Within years, the rare variant has swept to fixation.
It was not bacteria inventing resistance.
It was bacteria selecting it from an existing standing variation.
That is the Fleming scenario, and it is, in some form, the scenario that has played out for every antibiotic class since.
So let me put a sharper point on this.
There are two timescales of resistance evolution, and you need to keep them straight.
The first is mutational evolution within a lineage — a bacterium that didn't have resistance acquires it because of a new point mutation that happens to confer it.
This is slow-ish — you need the right mutation, you need it to arise in a viable cell, you need selection to amplify it.
The second is horizontal gene transfer — a bacterium acquires a fully-formed, pre-evolved resistance gene from another bacterium, possibly of a completely different species, by direct DNA exchange.
This is fast, because the gene already exists, somewhere else, in working order, and the recipient is just picking it up off the shelf.
The penicillinase story is, in part, the second kind.
The gene already existed in some bacterial lineage.
It moved onto a mobile element.
The mobile element traveled into Staph aureus.

The clock from the drug's deployment to widespread resistance is essentially the clock of horizontal gene transfer plus selective sweep, which is fast — much faster than anyone reasoning from pure mutational evolution would have expected.

Fleming may not have known about plasmids and conjugation in the way we do now — that mechanistic detail came later, in the 1950s and 1960s — but his intuition about the speed of selection was already right.
He knew the bug was smarter than the drug.
We deployed the drug.
The bug got smart on the schedule he predicted.
Now let's go look, in molecular detail, at exactly what "getting smart" means.
Four mechanisms.
Pick any antibiotic resistance gene in any pathogen, and it will be one of these four.
